title: First Instrument & Musical Play (Recorder or Starter Instrument, Basic Notation, Ensemble Basics)

required.title: [Study] A beginner method book for the chosen instrument
required.author: John Pitts or equivalent. The book should progress from single notes to simple melodies to easy duets.

required.title: [For Mentor] A pitch reference and the ability to demonstrate

suggested.title: [Experience] Instrument petting zoo or demo day
[Listen Together] Recordings featuring the child's instrument
suggested.author: Telemann, Handel, or Vivaldi (the recorder was a respected concert instrument in the Baroque era). This shows the child that their "beginner" instrument has a real musical history.

competence_markers.description: The child can produce a clear, steady tone on the recorder (or chosen starter instrument)
The child knows fingerings for at least eight notes and can transition between them smoothly
The child can read simple notation on the treble clef staff and translate it into correct pitches on the instrument
The child can play at least four simple melodies from beginning to end with steady rhythm and clear notes
The child can play in unison or simple duet with another player, maintaining tempo and listening to the other part
The child practices daily with a consistent routine
The child has explored instruments from at least three different families and can articulate preferences
The child continues to sing regularly alongside instrument practice

narration_prompts.prompt: Play me a melody you have learned. What was the hardest part about learning it? What did you practice to get it right?
narration_prompts.detail: 2. **What did you have to learn about your breath to make a good sound on the recorder? What happens when you blow too hard? Too soft?**

narration_prompts.prompt: What is it like playing music with another person? What do you have to do that you do not have to do when you play alone?
narration_prompts.detail: 4. **Of all the instruments you have tried or heard, which one do you want to learn more about? Why?**

cross_connections.description: Every melody has a rhythm. The steady beat and rhythmic pattern skills from 3.4.1 are applied directly when playing melodies in time.
Notation reading connects the ear training of 3.4.2 to the physical production of sound. The child is translating written symbols into sounds — the same skill that reading text develops for language.

Daily instrument practice is a habit. The discipline of practicing when you do not feel like it, of repeating difficult passages, and of maintaining a consistent schedule exercises the same habit-formation skills developed in IC2.
Reading music notation and reading text are parallel literacy skills. Both involve decoding symbols into meaning. The child developing both simultaneously builds general decoding fluency.
An instrument is a tool. Caring for it (cleaning the recorder after playing, storing it properly) is an entry point for the tool care habits developed in 2.2.

content.why_this_matters:
You have been singing, and your voice is stronger and more confident than it was when you started. Now you are going to learn to make music with something besides your voice — an instrument.

An instrument is a tool for turning your musical ideas into sound. When you play an instrument, you must coordinate your breath, your fingers, your eyes (reading music), and your ears (listening to what you produce) all at the same time. This is complex, and it takes practice. But the reward is enormous: the ability to produce music from an object in your hands, to play melodies and songs, and to make music with other people.

In this packet, you will learn a starter instrument — most likely the soprano recorder, which is inexpensive, portable, and an excellent teacher of breath control, finger coordination, and basic pitch production. You will also begin reading simple musical notation — the written language of music that allows you to learn new pieces without having someone sing them to you first. And you will experience playing music with others, which requires everything you have learned so far plus one crucial new skill: listening while you play.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Instrumental tone production** — making a clear, controlled sound on a starter instrument
2. **Finger coordination** — learning fingerings and developing the motor skills to change notes smoothly
3. **Basic notation reading** — translating written notes on a staff into physical actions on the instrument
4. **Simple melody performance** — playing recognizable tunes from start to finish
5. **Breath control** (for wind instruments) — the physical skill of controlling airflow to produce steady, clear tone
6. **Ensemble awareness** — playing a part while listening to others, the foundation of all group music-making
7. **Instrument exploration** — exposure to multiple instrument families to inform future instrument selection
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Making Your First Sound

Before you can play a melody, you need to make a single, clean, clear note.

On the recorder, this means: cover the holes completely with the pads of your fingers (not the tips — the soft pads). Support the instrument with your thumbs. Place the mouthpiece gently between your lips. Blow softly — not hard. The recorder responds to gentle, steady airflow. Blowing too hard makes a squeaky, harsh sound. Blowing just right makes a warm, clear tone.

The first note most recorder methods teach is B (left thumb on the back hole, left index finger on the first hole). Play it. Hold it for four beats. Listen to the sound. Is it clear or squeaky? If it is squeaky, blow more gently and make sure the holes are fully covered.

This is the first lesson about instruments that applies to every instrument you will ever play: the quality of your sound matters as much as the notes you play. A beautiful tone on a single note is worth more than a sloppy melody on twenty notes.

### Part 2: Adding Notes

Once you can play B cleanly, you learn A (add the second finger) and G (add the third finger). Three notes. With three notes, you can play simple melodies: "Hot Cross Buns" (B-A-G, B-A-G, G-G-G-G, A-A-A-A, B-A-G) and other three-note songs.

Each new note requires a new fingering — a new combination of covered and uncovered holes. Each new fingering requires practice until the fingers move smoothly and the transition between notes is clean, not bumpy. This is motor learning, and it happens through repetition: play the transition between B and A twenty times. Then between A and G. Then B to G. Then B-A-G in sequence. Slow practice, repeated daily, builds the finger memory that eventually makes playing automatic.

Over the course of this packet, you will learn enough notes to play in the key of C major (C-D-E-F-G-A-B-C) and a few additional notes. This gives you access to a large number of simple melodies: folk songs, hymns, and exercises from your method book.

### Part 3: Reading Music — Notes on the Staff

You began learning about notation in 3.4.1 (rhythmic notation) and 3.4.2 (the staff, lines and spaces). Now you will put it all together: reading notes that tell you both which pitch to play and how long to hold it.

On the treble clef staff:
- **E** sits on the first (bottom) line
- **F** sits in the first space
- **G** sits on the second line
- **A** sits in the second space
- **B** sits on the third line

And so on upward. Your method book will introduce notation gradually, one note at a time, always connecting the written note to the fingering on your instrument and the sound it produces.

Reading music is like reading words: at first, you decode letter by letter (note by note), slowly. With practice, you begin to read groups of notes as phrases, just as you read groups of letters as words. Fluency comes with consistent practice. Read a little every day.

### Part 4: Simple Melodies

The goal of this packet is to play recognizable melodies from beginning to end. Not fast. Not fancy. But complete and musical — with a steady beat, clear notes, and enough control that a listener can recognize the tune.

Your method book will provide a progression of melodies. In addition, work on songs you already know from singing: "Twinkle, Twinkle, Little Star," "Mary Had a Little Lamb," "Ode to Joy." The experience of playing a song you have been singing creates a powerful connection between the voice, the ear, and the instrument.

When learning a new melody:
1. Look at the notation. Identify the notes and the rhythm.
2. Sing the melody first (you already know how to do this).
3. Finger through the notes slowly without blowing.
4. Play the melody slowly, one phrase at a time.
5. Connect the phrases.
6. Play the whole melody at a comfortable tempo.
7. Repeat daily until it is smooth and confident.

### Part 5: Playing with Others

Playing music with other people is different from playing alone. When you play alone, you only have to keep track of your own part. When you play with others, you must listen to what they are playing and adjust your timing, your volume, and your part to fit with theirs.

Start simple: the mentor claps a steady beat while the child plays a melody. Then: two children play the same melody together (unison). Then: one plays the melody while another holds a drone (a single sustained note). Then: simple duets from the method book, where two players play different parts that fit together.

Ensemble playing is where all the skills come together: rhythm (staying in time with the group), listening (hearing the other parts), tone (blending your sound with others), and musicianship (making the whole sound better than any individual part).

### Part 6: Exploring Other Instruments

Before you choose a primary instrument in Weathering Storms, you should have some experience with instruments beyond the recorder. Where possible, try:
- **Piano or keyboard** — press keys, hear the immediate relationship between action and sound
- **Guitar or ukulele** — feel the strings vibrate under your fingers
- **Violin or cello** — hear the singing quality of a bowed string
- **Trumpet or other brass** — discover that buzzing your lips can make a powerful sound
- **Drum or percussion** — revisit the instruments from 3.4.1 with new appreciation

You do not need lessons on each. You need the experience of holding them, making sound on them, and feeling which ones draw you. When Weathering Storms asks you to commit to a primary instrument, you should choose from experience, not from guessing.
content.formative_practices:
### Activity 1: Daily Practice [Ongoing — Every Day]
Practice the recorder (or chosen instrument) for 10-15 minutes daily. This is non-negotiable. Short, focused, daily practice builds skill faster than long, infrequent sessions. The practice should follow the method book's progression and include review of previously learned material.

### Activity 2: First Three Notes [Early Packet]
Master B, A, and G. Play each note cleanly for four beats. Then play transitions: B to A, A to G, B to G. Then play "Hot Cross Buns" and other three-note melodies. Do not add new notes until these three are solid.

### Activity 3: Notation Reading [Ongoing]
Read and play exercises from the method book. Connect each written note to its fingering and its sound. The goal is fluency: eventually the child should see a note on the page and produce it on the instrument without conscious thought about fingering.

### Activity 4: Known Songs on the Instrument [Mid-Packet]
Transfer songs the child already knows from singing to the instrument. "Twinkle, Twinkle, Little Star," "Mary Had a Little Lamb," and others. The child already knows the melody by ear — now they learn to produce it with their fingers.

### Activity 5: Duets and Ensemble [Mid-to-Late Packet]
Play simple duets with the mentor, a sibling, or another student. Start with unison playing. Progress to simple two-part arrangements. The emphasis is on listening and staying together, not on complexity.

### Activity 6: Instrument Exploration Day [Late Packet]
Arrange opportunities for the child to try instruments from different families. A music store, a school open house, a visit from a musician friend, or online demonstrations. The child should identify at least two instruments they would like to explore further in Weathering Storms.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Tone Quality
**Developing well:** The child produces a clear, steady sound on most notes. The tone is warm rather than squeaky. Breath control is developing — the child can sustain a note for several beats without the sound wavering.

**Developing slowly:** The sound is consistently harsh, squeaky, or unsteady. The child blows too hard or cannot cover the holes completely.

**Diagnostic:** Check hand position (holes must be fully covered by finger pads, not tips). Check breath — the child may need to practice blowing gently through the instrument without fingering. Sometimes the instrument itself is defective — check that it produces clear tones when you play it.

### 2. Notation Reading
**Developing well:** The child reads simple notation and produces the correct pitches with minimal hesitation. Reading is becoming more fluid over time.

**Developing slowly:** The child must stop and figure out each note individually, cannot connect notes into a phrase.

**Diagnostic:** More practice with fewer notes. If the child struggles with a full octave, go back to three or four notes and practice reading exercises using only those notes until recognition is instant. Then add one note at a time.

### 3. Practice Habit
**Developing well:** The child practices daily without significant resistance. They have a routine and can work through method book exercises with decreasing supervision.

**Developing slowly:** The child resists practice, does not practice unless reminded, or goes through the motions without focus.

**Diagnostic:** Is the practice session too long? Too boring? Too difficult? Shorten sessions if needed. Include a "fun" component — playing a song the child chose, improvising, or playing duets. Make sure the child is experiencing success, not just frustration.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Stopping Singing
When the instrument arrives, singing should not stop. The child should continue to sing daily alongside instrument practice. Singing and playing develop different but complementary skills, and the child who stops singing loses the vocal foundation built in A1.

### Rushing Through the Method Book
The temptation to advance quickly through the book is strong, especially if the child is eager. Resist it when quality suffers. Each lesson should be mastered — not just attempted — before moving on. A solid foundation of clean tone and confident reading on a few notes is worth more than hasty progress through many notes with poor quality.

### Neglecting the Ensemble Experience
Playing alone in practice is necessary. But the child must also experience playing with others — even if "others" is just you clapping a rhythm while they play, or playing unison melodies together. Ensemble playing is where music-making becomes social, and that social dimension is essential.
